content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Media monitoring used to mean a Google Alert and a lot of hoping. Now every PR tech vendor is selling “AI-powered sentiment analysis,” and most of it oversells what it can actually do.
Here’s what I’ve found actually holds up, and where I’d stay skeptical.
Where AI genuinely helps
Volume triage. If your brand or client is mentioned across hundreds of articles, posts, and forums a day, AI is legitimately good at sorting the pile flagging spikes, surfacing outliers, and grouping similar coverage so you’re not reading everything manually. This is the single biggest time-saver in the workflow.
First-pass sentiment tagging. AI can label mentions as broadly positive, negative, or neutral fast enough to spot a shift in tone before it becomes a headline. Useful as an early warning system.
Summarizing narrative themes. Instead of reading 40 articles about a product launch, AI can pull out the 3–4 recurring angles journalists are taking, which is genuinely useful for briefing leadership.
Where I’d push back on the hype
Sentiment scoring is not nuance detection. AI regularly misreads sarcasm, industry-specific context, and mixed-sentiment coverage (an article can be critical of a decision but complimentary of the CEO’s response most tools flatten that into one score). Treat AI sentiment as a directional signal, not a verdict.
It cannot replace judgment on what actually matters. A story with low engagement in a top-tier outlet can matter more than a viral post in a low-credibility one. AI tools weight this inconsistently. That’s still a comms person’s call.
Hallucination risk on context. If you ask an AI tool to summarize “why” sentiment shifted, it will sometimes invent a plausible-sounding reason that isn’t actually supported by the coverage. Always verify the “why,” not just the “what.”
A workflow that’s worked for me

• Use AI monitoring tools for the first pass, flagging volume spikes and rough sentiment across all mentions.

• Manually review anything flagged as a strong shift (positive or negative); don’t trust the score alone.

• Use AI to summarize narrative themes across the flagged set, then verify the summary against 3–5 source articles.

• Only escalate to leadership once you’ve confirmed the “why” behind a shift, not just the “what.”
A prompt you can copy-paste
If you’re pulling monitoring data into a document or spreadsheet and want a fast first-pass read, try this:
“Here is a list of media mentions from the last 24 hours [paste headlines/excerpts]. Group them into 3–4 recurring narrative themes. For each theme, note the general sentiment direction and flag any mention where sentiment seems mixed or context-dependent rather than clearly positive or negative.”
This keeps the AI doing triage, not making the final call, which is where it belongs.
The bottom line
AI monitoring tools are a real time-saver for volume and first-pass triage. They are not a replacement for someone who understands the difference between “this article uses negative words” and “this article is actually bad for us.” That judgment is still the job.
Have you used AI monitoring tools in your workflow? What’s worked and what’s burned you?
